judgments.title: WAYTHAMOORTHY A/L PONNUSAMY v PENERBITAN SAHABAT (M) SDN BHD & ors
judgments.catchwords: TORT: Defamation — Libel — Newspaper article — Tamil daily — Article portraying Plaintiff as a "saboteur" attempting to stop the Thaipusam festival — Whether words in their natural and ordinary meaning defamatory — Whether words referred to Plaintiff — Whether words published to third parties — Translation dispute — Meaning of Tamil word "Vettu" — Whether interpretations of "sabotage", "obstruct", or "incite" defamatory per se — "Defamatory half-truth" — Selective editing of Plaintiff's open letter to suppress material context of public health concerns — Whether Article constituted an attack on Plaintiff's moral character. TORT: Defamation — Defences — Fair Comment — Requirements — Whether comment based on facts — Editorial invention of "saboteur" narrative — Whether characterisation of motives as political had factual foundation — Whether defeated by express malice — Reckless indifference to truth. TORT: Defamation — Defences — Qualified Privilege — Common and corresponding interest — Whether defeated by express malice — Particulars of malice — Rules of Court 2012, Order 78 Rule 3(3) — Creation of derogatory pseudonym "Vettu Vaikkum Waytha Moorthy" — Use of large red fonts to signal targeted campaign — Failure to retract or apologise despite Letter of Demand. TORT: Defamation — Defences — Reynolds Privilege — Responsible journalism — Factors to consider — Seriousness of allegation — Nature of information — Source of information — Steps taken to verify — Urgency — Whether comment sought from Plaintiff — Whether article contained "gist" of Plaintiff’s side of story — Failure to include public health context of open letter — Lack of verifiable evidence of alleged "nationwide condemnation". CIVIL PROCEDURE: Pleadings — Defamation — Adequacy of Statement of Claim — Failure to identify precise words complained of — Verbatim reproduction of entire article — Whether Defendants caught off-guard or prejudiced — Whether technical objections sustainable at close of trial. EVIDENCE: Adverse inference — Evidence Act 1950, s. 114(g) — Failure to call temple administrators or produce reporter notes to substantiate claim of public anger — Whether adverse inference drawn against Defendants. DAMAGES: Assessment — Public figure — Former Federal Minister and Senator — Position and standing — Gravity of "sting" — Allegations of being a traitor to the Hindu community — Mode and extent of publication — Conduct of Defendants — Lack of remorse — General damages — Aggravated damages. REMEDIES: Injunction — Restraining publication already in public domain — Whether exercise in futility. REMEDIES: Mandatory apology — Whether Court has jurisdiction to compel a defendant to publish an apology — Absence of statutory or common law foundation — Apology as a voluntary act — Compelled speech — Whether Mandatory Order for Rectification and Factual Withdrawal available under inherent jurisdiction.
judgments.judge: YA Tuan Avinder Singh Gill a/l Ranjit Singh

judgments.title: VIGILANT VENTURES SDN BHD v Lembaga Kemajuan Ikan Malaysia (LKIM)
judgments.catchwords: Contract Law – Formation and interpretation – Collaboration Agreement – Duration clause – Intention for 30 years subject to detailed extension mechanism – Trial period and automatic extensions – Further extensions require mutual written agreement – No unilateral right to compel extension after second automatic extension Contract Law – Expiry by effluxion of time – Distinguished from termination for breach – Agreement expiring by its own terms does not require notice of default or termination – Contract simply comes to an end Contract Law – Fraud and misrepresentation – Contract induced by fraud remains valid until rescinded – Finding of fraud relevant to damages, not to contractual expiry – Not contradictory to finding that contract was lawfully entered into Contract Law – Damages – Special damages – Must be strictly pleaded and strictly proved – Supported by documentary evidence (invoices, receipts, accounts) – Loss of guaranteed profits – Failure to prove actual gross income – Award set aside Contract Law – Damages – General damages – Global award of RM2.4 million – No proper legal basis pleaded (account of profits/unjust enrichment not pleaded) – No strict proof of actual loss – Award set aside Contract Law – Damages – Election between reliance loss and expectation loss – Claimant must elect – Cannot claim both Land Law – Vacant possession – Holding over after expiry of agreement – Registered proprietor entitled to vacant possession – Order affirmed

judgments.catchwords: "Full trial of consolidated suits by Management Corporation and Purchasers of condominium units seeking to declare a Sale and Purchase agreement between Developer and another as void and to transfer what they consider common property to the Management Committee. - Issues interalia are whether fraud has been established and the registered title to the property indefeasible; whether the property and accessory car parks constitute common property; whether the Developer has breached the SPA's; whether the MC has the requisite locus standi; and whether the Defendants counter claim for tresspass proven? - Court makes the following findings :- 1. the MC lacks the requisite locus standi but the Purchases have so; 2. the Plaintiffs have failed to establish fraud; 3. the property is not common property as determined by reference to the Certified Strata Plans and Schedule of Parcels; 4. no breach of the DOs or SPA's have been establish in that there is no shortage of car parks; 5. counter claim dismissed as no evidence of tresspass. judgments.catchwords: Construction Industry Payment and Adjudication Act 2012 – a question of considerable Doctrinal importance at the intersection of construction law and insolvency law: what is the effect, upon a winding-up order against a company which was founded upon an adjudication decision obtained by that company’s creditor, of a subsequent final determination by the court which conclusively reverses that adjudication decision on the merits - does the winding up order — by itself, and notwithstanding that the adjudication decision upon which it was founded has been extinguished under s. 13(c) of CIPAA by the final court determination — have the effect of transmuting the adjudication decision into a binding, permanent debt which survives that final determination, and which the CIPAA claimant may thereafter invoke as a mutual set-off under s. 526 of the Companies Act 2016 (“CA 2016”) against a winding-up petition subsequently presented by the aggrieved debtor company whose claims were vindicated in those very court proceedings? - the meaning of “finally decided by arbitration or the court” in s. 13(c), whether final determination means the decision must have been “decided, and no longer open to any form of challenge or appeal.
judgments.judge: YA Dato' Ong Chee Kwan
coram: YA Datuk Seri Mohd Firuz Bin Jaffril
YA Dato' Ong Chee Kwan
YA Tuan Dean Wayne Daly

judgments.catchwords: Administrative Law – Judicial review – Domestic tribunal – Voluntary association – Golf club – Disciplinary proceedings – Suspension of club membership – Originating Summons – Scope of judicial intervention – Whether Court should interfere with disciplinary decision of club – Whether Court acts as appellate tribunal on merits – Whether material breach of natural justice established – Whether material breach of Club Bye-Laws established – Whether decision unlawful, ultra vires or null and void – Plaintiff's application dismissed. Club Law – Membership of club – Contractual relationship between club and member – Constitution and Bye-Laws governing disciplinary proceedings – Compliance with disciplinary procedure – Interpretation of Bye-Law 7(c), Bye-Law 8(a), Bye-Law 9 and Bye-Law 12(c) – Meaning of phrase "within 3 working days or as soon as practicable" – Whether "as soon as practicable" prescribes a fixed period – Whether delay of 81 days invalidated disciplinary proceedings – Whether actual prejudice established. Natural Justice – Audi alteram partem – Right to be heard – Adequate notice of complaint – Show cause notice – Opportunity to respond – Written explanation – Attendance at disciplinary inquiry – Mitigation – Internal appeal – Whether Plaintiff denied fair hearing – Whether procedural irregularities caused actual prejudice – Distinction between procedural imperfections and material denial of natural justice. Evidence – Affidavit evidence – Failure to produce documents – Discovery – Investigation reports – Inquiry notes – Minutes – Witness statements – Adverse inference under section 114(g) Evidence Act 1950 – Whether adverse inference should be drawn – Whether non-disclosure rendered disciplinary proceedings invalid. Practice and Procedure – Originating Summons – Declaratory relief – Burden of proof – Judicial restraint – Court not to substitute its own assessment of evidence for that of domestic tribunal – Relief refused – Costs awarded to Defendant.

judgments.title: SIVACHANDRAN A/L S JAYARAJAH v Pendakwa Raya
judgments.catchwords: Review jurisdiction - Rule 137 of the Rules of the Federal Court 1995 -Inherent powers of Federal Court – All sentences passed to be in accordance with the law – Avoiding miscarriage of justice - Exceptional circumstances - Fresh evidence of prisoner’s death following whipping. Constitutional Law - Federal Constitution - Articles 5(1), 8(1) and 8(2) Fundamental liberties - Life and personal liberty - Human dignity - Equal protection of the law - Whether it is in violation of medical ethics for medical officer to be participate in the execution of whipping. Judicial whipping - Corporal punishment - Whether judicial whipping amounts to cruel, inhuman and degrading treatment (“CIDT”) - Whether punishment of whipping is inconsistent with constitutional protection of life, dignity and bodily integrity. Whether whipping is disproportionate and therefore contrary to Article 8(1) - Whether exemption of women from whipping constitutes prohibited discrimination on ground only of gender is contrary to Art 8(2) - Intelligible differentia. Whether whipping is against Art. 5 of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (“UDHR”) and the ASEAN Human Rights Declaration in its prohibitions against CIDT - Whether whipping is against customary international law.
judgments.judge: YAA Tun Wan Ahmad Farid Bin Wan Salleh

judgments.title: NOMALAN A/L RAMAYAH v Pendakwa Raya
judgments.catchwords: Abolition of Mandatory Death Penalty Act 2023 – Child sentence under Section 97(2) of the Child Act 2001- "detained in prison during the pleasure of the Yang di-Pertuan Agong (King) or Ruler"- does not violate Article 5 because the child offender is convicted after the due process of a trial and at its conclusion, the sentence passed is in accordance with a validly passed law- Doctrine of reasonable classification- doctrine of proportionality-ultimate objective was to place child offenders in a category of their own so as to not suffer the extreme fate of adult offenders with the view to being given an opportunity at rehabilitation and their eventual re-integration back into society- reasonable nexus that existed between the discrimination and that legislative aim sought to be achieved-no violation or fundamental breach of Article 8-alternative remedies provided for under the law for the Applicants to be dealt with according to Section 97(4) of the Child Act 2001, Regulation 54 and 113 of the Prison Regulations 2000 read together with power of pardon under Article 42 of the Federal Constitution-remedy afforded exclusively to the Applicants via section 97(5) of the Child Act 2001 provides the opportunity for them to be released earlier from detention, by virtue of the yearly recommendation by the Board of Visiting Justices. This opportunity is not afforded to a convict of the age of majority- Section 2 and Section 3 of Act 847 makes in clear that the pre-condition for the application under Section 3 is that the person must be on death row at the date of enforcement of Act 847- Applicants’ squarely outside the purview of Act 847
judgments.judge: YA Dato' Collin Lawrence Sequerah
coram: YAA Tun Wan Ahmad Farid Bin Wan Salleh
YAA Datuk Hajah Azizah binti Haji Nawawi

YA Dato' Che Mohd Ruzima Bin Ghazali

judgments.title: Majlis Bandaraya Alor Setar v SOMASUNDRAM A/L ARUMUGAM
judgments.catchwords: Tort: Negligence — Statutory negligence — Local authority — Breach of common law duty of care — Road accident — Obstruction on public thoroughfare — Mobile metal barrier used for bazaar crowd control — Duty to ensure road safety vs. road user’s reciprocal duty to maintain a proper lookout. Tort: Negligence — Contributory negligence — Apportionment of liability — Motorist failing to avoid large, stationary, and brightly colored object in broad daylight — Stationary object already present on road — Inapplicability of 'agony of the moment' doctrine — Failure to demonstrate baseline judgment and vehicular control — Liability divided equally (50:50). Civil Procedure: Appeal — Appellate intervention — Standard of review — Manifest deficit in judicial appreciation of primary facts — Findings inconsistent with objective physical evidence — Whether trial court's decision was "plainly wrong". Evidence: Burden of proof — Balance of probabilities — Doctrine of res ipsa loquitur — Inapplicability where competing and equally plausible explanations for the accident coexist — Doctrine as a rule of evidence, not a substitute for primary proof of causation — Section 114(g) Evidence Act 1950 — Adverse inference — Failure to call key eyewitness pillion rider — Factual improbability of impact based on intact state of barrier — Prejudicial delay in reporting accident. Damages: General damages — Personal injury — Quantum — Assessment — Head injury — Subdural haemorrhage — Proof of damages — Requirement for cogent evidence — Improper reliance on medical report containing explicit caveat for further specialist surgical input — Failure to call treating surgical specialist — Whether award was manifestly excessive compared to established judicial trends.
